Skip to the questions
Stars & Bears All notes  ·  Economy
The life of a $100
The big picture The GDP cake Jobs & pay Profits Pick your state What's "worth"?
Our money at home The family budget Spending over time Spending behaviors Where you live
The big forces Supply & demand Will AI cut costs? So… the economy?
Sources Thanks & inspiration
🧒 A bedtime Q&A

Our Economy Boat: At Shore

My kiddo kept asking the big questions at dinner. So I asked Claude to answer them — with real charts you can actually poke at.

Starring an 8-Year-Old  •  answers & graphs by Claude

Last updated: 07/13/2026  ·  estimated reading time: 25 mins to 1 hr

🧒
First, the big questionWhere does our money come from?
✦

Money doesn't grow on trees — it gets born!

Dollars are born in two nurseries. The famous one: when the economy requires, the Federal Reserve (the Fed) creates brand-new dollars. It doesn't just hand them out, though: it trades them to banks for bonds. That's nursery #1 — and it isn't even the big one!

Nursery #2 is an ordinary bank. When a bank gives a loan — say, $100 to open a lemonade stand — it doesn't take that $100 out of anyone's savings. It types a brand-new $100 into the borrower's account. And it's the very same trade the Fed makes — brand-new money swapped for a debt: a promise of good work to come and a trust in the future — it lets tomorrow's lemonade stand open today. Most of America's money was born this way — lent into existence. The economic confidence sets the lending pace.

Put the two nurseries together, and here is money's whole life in one picture. Click the picture to zoom in.

Note: yes, this is really how it works — see the Bank of England's “Money creation in the modern economy” (2014 Q1).

🧒
But wait — was the erased money wasted?
✦

Well, that's the flowing nature of money.

While the "$100 💸" lived, it got exchanged, hand by hand, into other valuables — the house that got built (physical capital), the shop that opened (productive capital), the skills someone learned (human capital), the money that helped more people trade (flowing capital) and the bond that built the bridge (financial capital).

🧒
Money pilesSo with all this borning and vanishing… how much money is alive right now?
✦

About $22 trillion — and look at what all the finished lives left behind.

Count every dollar alive right now — cash in wallets, plus what's in checking and savings accounts — and you get about $22 trillion. Grown-ups call this pile M2.

But the money pile isn't the whole story — remember what dollars do before they vanish. All those finished lifetimes got traded into assets: things that keep their value — houses, businesses, savings set aside for later. Add up everything American families own, subtract what they still owe, and you get about $180 trillion of net worth — eight times the money pile! Dollars come and go; the value they build up stays.

Money alive (M2) next to the value it helped build (families' net worth), each year, in trillions. That steep 2020 climb in M2 is the pandemic money boost. Hover the lines.

Note: annual averages. M2 = FRED M2SL; net worth = Fed Z.1 B.101, households & nonprofits — everything owned (houses, businesses, stocks, deposits) minus everything owed. Data in data/q0-money/m2-vs-net-worth.csv.

🧒
Follow the moneyTwenty-two TRILLION dollars?! Okay… who's actually holding it?
✦

Mostly, it's sitting in families' accounts.

Line up everyone's ready money — wallets, checking, savings and money-market accounts — and nearly two-thirds of it belongs to households like yours — about 63%. Nonprofits — churches, charities, museums, universities — keep about 3%. Businesses big and small hold about a fifth (their payroll and rainy-day cash). Governments (federal + state + local) keep about 6%. And roughly 7% sits with the rest of the world: people and companies abroad who like saving in dollars.

Who holds America's ready money — cash plus checking, savings & money-market accounts (Fed Z.1 Financial Accounts, Q1 2026). Hover a bar.

But wait — where are the banks? 🏦 Trick question! A deposit is your money and the bank's debt — so banks can't be on the holders' list at all. And the small government slices on the chart are what governments keep at ordinary banks. Governments don't sit on mountains of cash. The mountain of savings is ours.

~63¢of every $1 — held by families
~21¢businesses' checkbooks
~6¢all governments combined

Note: this chart slices the Fed's holder-side accounts (Z.1), which count a bit more than headline M2 ($31T vs $23T) — companies' large time-deposits and institutional money funds show up in the sector data but not in M2's definition. The shares are of the holder-side total; the data and exact series are in data/q0-money/money-holders.csv.

🧒
Rich yet?So does that giant $22 trillion pile mean our country is rich?
✦

Not really! A country isn't rich because money sits in its piggy bank — it's rich because of the giant cake it bakes every year: GDP.

To measure how rich a country is, we don't count the cash lying around. That pile is just our ready money. The treasure it left behind — net worth — is one answer: what we own. But economists' favorite ruler counts something livelier: all the new stuff and useful work everyone makes in a year (houses built, meals served, apps written, patients cared for). That grand total is Gross Domestic Product (GDP) — about $30.8 trillion for the US, and we picture it as one giant cake to share.

🧒
Question 1Then… what are the flavors of the cake?
✦

Great question. The $30.8 trillion cake has 20 layers — each with its own flavor.

Think of every kind of business as one layer of the cake, with its own flavor — housing, health care, food, computers and books. A layer's thickness is how much value it adds. The surprise? The thickest layer isn't toys, or even computers — it's houses and apartments (a flavor we call "real estate"), because everybody needs a place to live.

$30.8Tsize of the cake (2025)
Real estatethickest layer · 14%
20different flavors
Each rectangle is one layer of the cake — one flavor. Hover or tap it to taste what's inside.

👆 Touch any flavor above to find out what that part of the economy actually does — and some companies you might know.

Has the cake always tasted like this? Nope — the recipe slowly changes. Over the last 20 years the "making things" layer got thinner while the "services & ideas" layers got thicker. Manufacturing slid from about 13% of the cake down to 9%, while professional & tech work, health care and information all climbed.

Each line is one big layer's share of the whole cake, every year from 2005 to 2025. Sliding down = a thinning layer; climbing = a thickening one. Hover a line.

Those are the layers as shares of the cake. In plain dollars, every layer grew, because the whole cake swelled from about $13 trillion in 2005 to $30.8 trillion in 2025. Even manufacturing's thinning share means more dollars than before (about $1.7T → $2.9T) — its layer grew slower than the cake; it didn't shrink. And remember: part of every line's climb is just prices going up over the years (inflation), not only more stuff.

The same six layers, now in dollars of new value per year instead of shares. Every line climbs — these are each year's own dollars (not inflation-adjusted), so part of the climb is rising prices. Hover a line.
🧒
Question 2So who bakes all these flavors?
✦

The grown-ups! Baking a layer of this cake is exactly why they go to work every day. Before we meet the bakers, peek inside the kitchen. The layers of the cake are baked on top of each other: every layer buys its ingredients from other layers — the car factory buys steel from the mill, the restaurant buys vegetables from the farm. That chain of layers is a supply chain, and here's the cool part: because everything travels down one before it reaches you, way more than $22 trillion changes hands every year — over $50 trillion! Fig. 7 shows how the money flows from the downstream consumers to the upstream industries. Add up every sale at every stop, across every industry, and you get gross output — all the money flowing through.

But not all of that money is new. Only the new value a layer bakes on top of the ingredients it bought is truly its own — that's value added: a layer's sales minus what it buys from the other layers, so nothing gets counted twice. America's factories all together sell about $7.3 trillion of stuff but buy about $4.4 trillion of metal, chips and parts, so their value added is about $2.9 trillion. Stack up every industry's new value — layer on layer — and you get GDP, the whole cake — Fig. 8 compares the two for every big industry. 🍰

Fig. 7 · $30,000, four stops: hopping backwards up the chain, and a register rings at every hop. Brown arrows = all the money that flowed through (gross output); dark green boxes = the value added kept at each stop.
Fig. 8 · For each industry: the whole river of money flowing through it (gross output, light green) vs. the new value it adds (dark green) — which is its layer of the cake. See how the factory's river is huge but its layer is thinner? The % on each dark bar shows how much of every $1 flowing through stays as new value. Hover a bar.
~$22Tall the money there is (M2)
~$53Tmoney flowing through industries (gross output)
~$30.8Tnew value kept = the cake (GDP)

Wait — how can $53T flow through industries when only $22T of money exists? Because money doesn't get used up when it's spent! The dollar that buys your candy pays the store's cashier, who spends it at the barber, who buys groceries… Each dollar in the M2 pile rings a few different cash registers every year. Money (M2) is a pile; gross output and GDP are flows added up over a whole year — that's why the yearly flows can be much bigger than the pile.

And most of the bakers work on the caring-for-people flavors — not in factories or at computers.

About 156 million people work at America's employers — self-employed folks add several million more. The two biggest groups work in health care (doctors, nurses, dentists, care homes) and for the government (teachers, soldiers, police). Lots more work in stores and restaurants. Way fewer work at the famous "computer" companies than you'd guess.

How many people work in each part of the economy (millions of jobs, 2025). Same 20 flavors as the cake.

These jobs are grouped the exact same way as the cake's layers, so you can compare them fairly in the next question.

But do those jobs pay the same? Not even close. Some of the industries with the most workers — stores, restaurants and care work — are among the lowest paid, while information (tech), finance and company head offices pay two-to-three times more. The dashed line is the poverty line for a family of four (about $32,150 a year): almost every industry's average paycheck clears it — but the average restaurant & hotel paycheck, at $30,935, lands just below.

Average pay per worker in each industry (2025). Bars ending left of the dashed poverty line (family of four) don't clear it. Hover a bar.

Bars are the average yearly pay per worker (BLS QCEW, 2025). The poverty line is for a whole family of four (2025 HHS guideline); most homes have more than one earner, so this asks whether a single average paycheck could keep a family of four out of poverty. "Government" pay here is the public-administration sector — public teachers and hospital staff are counted under education and health care.

🧒
Question 3Why are paychecks so different across industries?
✦

Because some work makes a lot of value with very few people.

When we put each layer's share of the cake (value) next to its share of the jobs (people), some really stand out. Information (apps, phone, streaming) bakes about 5.5% of the cake with under 2% of the workers. Real estate is the wildest: the thickest layer, almost no bakers — because a house earns its "rent" just by existing. On the other side, restaurants, stores, and health care need lots and lots of people.

Red = share of the cake · Blue = share of the jobs. A tall red bar with a short blue bar means "big value, few people."

And there's a second part to why paychecks look so different — who keeps the money? A layer's value added is everything its whole team bakes together, and most of it flows right back out: paychecks for the bakers, taxes, worn-out ovens to replace, interest on the loan. Only what's left after every bill is paid belongs to the companies — that's profit.

For a grocery store, profit is about 1 cent of each sales dollar. Across all industries, the average is about 10 cents. Products and services that cost less to make a copy but are hard for others to copy — oftentimes requiring years or even decades of research, investment and commitment — keep more.

Profit: cents the companies keep out of each sales dollar — after paying suppliers, workers, rent and taxes — by type of business. The dashed line is the market average (~10¢).

Two different "keeps"! Value added (Fig. 8) is everything the whole team creates — paychecks included. Profit is only what's left for the companies after every paycheck and bill. High profit comes from things nobody else can copy (a secret recipe, a patent, a brand); low profit comes from selling what everyone else also sells.

One more secret hiding in both charts: every dollar a layer earns — the paychecks and the profit — had to walk in through the front door first. Somebody bought something. Trace any industry's earnings back far enough and you almost always land in the same place: a family choosing to spend. Grown-ups call that the consumer world 🛒 — and after a quick detour to your own state and one more question about what things are really worth, that's exactly where we're headed.

🧒
Pick your stateIs it the same everywhere? Does my state make and do the same stuff?
✦

Nope — every state has its own flavor. Pick one and watch the charts change!

The country's cake is really 50 little cakes added up, and each state's looks different — lots of farming in one, oil or tech or tourism in another. Choose a state below: first you'll see its industry mix side-by-side with the whole country — what it leans on more or less than average — then four charts show its value added, how its layers grew over 20 years, and its jobs and pay by industry. (M2 and gross output are national totals, so those two don't split by state.)

🇺🇸 United States: each industry's share of the economy (value added, 2025), next to the US average (grey bars). Longer colored bars show what your state leans on more than the country.
💰 Value each industry adds to the United States's cake (GDP, 2025).
🕰️ How the United States's layers grew, 2005–2025 (value added per year, in each year's own dollars — so part of every climb is inflation). The thickest layers start visible; click a name in the legend to show or hide any layer.
👷 How many people work in each industry in the United States (thousands of jobs, 2025).
💵 Average pay per worker in the United States (2025), next to the US average (grey bars); dashed line = that state's poverty line for a family of 4 (2025 guideline: $32,150 — higher in Alaska & Hawaii).

Same real data as the national charts, split by state — GDP from BEA (2025); jobs & pay from BLS QCEW (2025; private industries are private-sector, "Government" is federal + state + local). A few tiny industries in small states aren't shown on the jobs & pay charts because the government withholds very small counts to protect privacy. Press 📷 Save PNG on any chart to download it as a picture.

🧒
Question 4What makes an industry "worth" a lot — how much we buy from it, or how much we need it?
✦

Neither, exactly.

An industry's value now is defined as value added: what a layer sells, minus what it buys from the other layers.

What makes a layer thick? Money flowing in from everyone who wants what it makes — families shopping, businesses buying tools, other countries buying exports. Family shopping is only about two-thirds of the cake.

Here is how an average family's $101,805 paycheck spent (2023).

The tax piece here is income taxes (BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey, 2023 — the newest year BLS published a tax number). Families also pay Social Security, sales and other taxes that hide inside the other pieces.

And "need" is a different question from a different measuring tool. A price only tells you what someone will pay for one more of something — which depends on how scarce it is, not on how much we'd miss it if it vanished. So a layer's dollar size measures how much money moves through it, not how important it is. It's a tape measure, not a report card.

🧒
Question 5Wait — did you say two-thirds of the cake comes from us?
✦

Mostly, yes — follow a dollar upstream and you'll usually find a family's wallet.

Look back at the cake: about two-thirds of it — roughly 68¢ of every $1 — is households like yours buying things. Grown-ups call it consumer spending. The shop's money comes from shoppers; the factory's money comes from the shop; the trucker's money comes from the factory. The rest of the cake is businesses building tools for later, governments, and trade with the rest of the world. So the economy's main engine isn't a bank or a boardroom — it's kitchen tables like yours. Welcome to the consumer world. 🛒

Let's zoom into one of those kitchen tables. The average US household spends about $78,500 a year. The biggest bite by far is housing — about a third — then getting around (~17%), food (~13%), money set aside for savings & insurance (~12%), and health care (~8%). The first four bites — home, wheels, food and savings — eat about 74¢ of every dollar.

Every dollar the average family spends, split into slices (BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey, 2024). The five tan slices used to hide inside one big "Everything else" — now you can see what's in it. Hover a slice for the exact dollars.

"Housing" here is the whole home group: rent or mortgage, plus utilities and stuff for the house.

Note: consumer spending ÷ GDP ≈ 68% — 2025 quarterly average, BEA NIPA table 1.1.10 (FRED DPCERE1Q156NBEA). The other shares: business investment, government, and net exports.

🧒
Your turnWow, so many stuff! If I had $1,000 to spend... how should I split it?
✦

You tell me — try to spend $1,000 on what you value.

Forget prices for a minute. Split $1,000 by how much each thing matters to you — the roof, the rides, the food, the fun. Then press the button to see where a real family's money actually goes. The two won't match — and the gap is the interesting part.

Spent: $1,000 / $1,000 ✓ all done

Real numbers: how the average US household splits its spending — BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey, 2024 — shown as dollars per $1,000. "Savings & insurance" is money set aside, not spent; "Everything else" is school, gifts, haircuts and odds and ends.

🧒
Question 6Has it always been split like that?
✦

Nope — the slices slowly change shape.

Over 40 years, two slices grew and two shrank. Housing crept up from about 30% to 33% and health care nearly doubled its share (4.8% → 7.9%), while food eased from ~15% to ~13% and the clothes slice shrank by more than half (6% → 2.5%) as clothes got way cheaper. The family budget is its own little pizza — same pizza, different-sized slices.

Each line is one slice's share of family spending, 1984–2024 (BLS CES). Rising lines are eating more of the pizza.

But in real dollars? They shot up. 🚀 The slices only showed how the pizza is cut. Here's the whole pizza in real dollars, sliced into the same categories — about $22,000 per family in 1984, about $78,500 by 2024 (the top of the stack), roughly 3½ times as much — and every slice balloons, even though the shares barely moved.

The actual dollars each year, stacked into the same categories (BLS CES, 1984–2024). The top of the stack is total spending; every slice grows.

Why so many more dollars for the same shopping? Because prices climbed. A family often isn't buying more — it's paying more for the same things. That's inflation — and the CPI (Consumer Price Index) is the ruler grown-ups measure it with.

🧒
Question 7Will I spend money differently when I grow up?
✦

Yes — spending grows, peaks in your 40s–50s, then shrinks.

Spending climbs like a hill: small when you're young, biggest around age 45–54 (~$100,000 a year!), then lower in retirement. And the mix shifts — the young spend more on getting around, families on housing and food, and older people more and more on health care (its share grows from about 3% under 25 to over 14% at 75+).

And here's the part that sneaks up on you: as you grow up and (hopefully!) get richer, a smaller share of your money goes to the must-haves — a 170-year-old rule called Engel's law. Must-haves = food + shelter + utilities + health care - eating 60¢ to 40¢ of every $1 the family spends. And spending grows slower than income: the top fifth earns about 2.5× the average family's income but spends only about 1.9× as much — while the bottom fifth earns just a sixth of the average yet still spends nearly half as much.

Numbers: BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey, 2024 — Table 1101 (quintiles of income before taxes);

🧒
Question 8Does our family spend the same as families in other states?
✦

No — where you live changes both how much you spend and on what.

People in richer, pricier states spend more per person; people in cheaper states spend less. On this map, darker means more spending per person each year — the Northeast and West Coast glow darkest, the South is lighter.

Spending per person by state, 2024 (BEA). Darker = more. Hover a state. (Alaska & Hawaii left off to keep the map tidy.)

And the mix shifts too. Pick any state below to see how its spending splits across the big categories — housing takes a giant bite in expensive states, and the slices look different where homes are cheaper.

Per-person spending by category (BEA PCE by state, 2024), next to the US average (grey) when a state is picked. What's still in "Everything else": personal care, tobacco, magazines, jewelry, donations to churches & charities, and other odds and ends.

Heads up — a different ruler. This state chart uses BEA "PCE": spending per person, and it counts money spent for you too (like the part of a doctor bill your insurance or the government pays). So its numbers run bigger — especially health care — than the family-budget pizzas above, which come from a survey of what households pay out of their own pockets. It's the only spending data that exists for every state, so it's great for comparing places — just not an apples-to-apples match with the family budget.

🧒
Question 9Those spending slices from Question 6 keep bugging me… clothes kept getting cheaper, but housing and health care only got pricier. Why do some prices fall while others climb?
✦

Every price is a tug-of-war: how much people need a thing vs. how much of it there is.

When lots of families want something scarce — a home in a popular town, a doctor's visit — they end up bidding against each other, and the price climbs. When factories can churn out mountains of something — T-shirts, TVs — supply races ahead and the price falls. That's the secret behind those lines: clothes are easy to make more of; land and doctors aren't. So the cure for a high price is usually build more, not buy more.

Can we see the tug-of-war? 📊 Sort of: count the homes, cars and hospital beds the country has, next to the people who need them — all set to 100 in 2010. A line climbing above the grey "people" line means we made more than enough to go around; slipping below means scarcer per person — and that's where prices bite.

How much there is of each, compared with 2010 (2010 = 100). Grey dashed = people (the "need"). Each line runs to its source's latest year: people & homes 2026, cars 2023, hospital beds 2022. Sources: population & homes (Census / FRED), cars (FHWA), hospital beds (World Bank / OECD).

See it? 🚗 Cars raced ahead of people, so car prices rose gently. 🏠 Homes barely kept step — and they aren't built where everyone wants to live, so in popular cities they stay dear. 🏥 Hospital beds actually shrank while the country grew — a big reason medical prices keep climbing.

"People" is only a rough stand-in for demand — income, cheap borrowing, and where folks want to live matter too. But the picture is real: what we can mass-produce got roomier; what we can't got scarcer per person.

More buyers → ↑demand up, prices up
More supply → ↓build more, prices fall
🏠 Hard to build fast= stays pricey (scarce)
🧒
Question 10Everyone keeps talking about AI — could that make things cheaper?
✦

Some things, yes — AI is a super-fast helper that makes more with less.

Think of AI as a tireless helper that can do certain jobs much faster — writing, coding, answering questions, spotting patterns. When you can make more of something with less effort, that's exactly the "more supply" that pulls prices down (remember Question 9). It's the same reason clothes and gadgets got cheap — machines made mountains of them. So for information-y things — apps, help desks, translations, a lot of paperwork — AI could make them cheaper and better.

And here's a catch grown-ups argue about: when AI makes a company's work cheaper, do the savings turn into lower prices (if lots of companies compete) or bigger profits (if only a few do)? Today AI is a powerful and affordable supply booster — it'll make plenty of things cheaper and better. But it's a helper, not magic, so it still relies on humans.

🧒
Wow, that sounds fantastic!
✦

Mostly, yes! But falling prices come in two flavors — one is a gift, the other is a spiral.

Good deflation 🎁 is when prices fall because we can make more, cheaper — like clothes and TVs did (remember Question 6). Your dollar buys more every year; life gets better. If AI floods the world with cheaper goods and services, that's this kind — abundance. Falling prices from plenty are good news, not a crisis.

Bad deflation 🌀 is when prices fall because spending collapses — and it feeds on itself: people wait for lower prices → shops sell less → jobs and pay get cut → even less spending → prices fall further. Debts make it crueler: your loan stays the same size while your paycheck shrinks. That spiral is hard to escape.

So here's the real puzzle about AI: could we turn the gains into income and spending?

If we could make it through the night, we have a brighter day —

A city with an extra "AI mind" that cares. 🌆

Picture your city a little further down this road. Tiny sensors taste the water in every pipe and sniff the air on every block, while AI-assisted manufacturers churn out the low-carbon infrastructure that scrubs that air a little clearer every year; the moment something's off, the city fixes it — before anyone gets a cough. A health helper in every pocket keeps a gentle eye on grandpa's heart — and a billion other hearts at the same time — and waves a human doctor over at the first strange beat: the kind of everyone-gets-watched-over personalized care that isn't possible right now. A patient tutor for every kid that never runs out of new ways to explain fractions. Rides that look after their riders — walking a kid safely to school, kneeling for a senior, greeting a visitor in their own language. Smart power grids that save the windy night's electricity for the hot afternoon. Farms that give each plant exactly the sip it asks for, with AI keeping watch on the soil itself — the quiet pantry all our nutrients come from. Buoys and gliders listening to the sea — the cradle of our seafood and a treasure chest of climate clues. Scientists with AI microscopes hunting cures in months instead of decades. One more thing: it empowers us to pursue our interests, our creativity, and our freedom from social and financial obligations — helping open up whole new industries and markets that make the cake richer.

🧒
Last one, promiseSo… what is the economy, really?
✦

It's all of us, allocating our time and energy.

The economy is millions of people making things, helping each other, and choosing what's worth their time and care. Add up the choices and you get the cake (what we make), the jobs (who makes it), the profits (who keeps what), and the market (what we bet is coming next). No single boss decides the shape — we all do, a little at a time. Money comes and goes. It invites productivity, unites us to build things that take a million hands — things too precious even for money itself to hold. What it builds and brings into our lives stays with us. Not bad for a dinner-table question — and our economy boat hasn't even left the shore yet. ⛵

Where the numbers come from

Same real data as the grown-up report — just told as a bedtime story. All figures from primary US government and academic sources.

  • Where money comes from (the two nurseries): Bank of England, “Money creation in the modern economy” (Quarterly Bulletin 2014 Q1) — bank lending creates deposits; the money-lifecycle picture follows that mechanism.
  • Money piles (M2 & the treasure it left behind): M2 money stock from FRED M2SL (~$22T, annual average); families' net worth (~$180T) from the Fed's Z.1 Financial Accounts, table B.101 (FRED TNWBSHNO).
  • Follow the money (who holds it): Federal Reserve, Z.1 Financial Accounts, holder-side accounts by sector, Q1 2026. Context figures outside M2: bank reserves (FRED WRESBAL, ~$3.1T) and the Treasury General Account (FRED WTREGEN, ~$0.8T).
  • Question 1 (the cake & its layers, 2025): U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, GDP by Industry (value added, full-year 2025). How the layers changed, 2005–2025: same BEA series via FRED (quarterly series averaged to annual and divided by GDP) — Manufacturing fell from ~13% to 9.4% while professional/tech, health care, finance and information rose.
  • Question 2 (the bakers: jobs, pay & the poverty line): U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, QCEW — employment and average annual pay per worker by NAICS sector, 2025 (Accommodation & food services $30,935; Information $184,220). Poverty line = 2025 HHS poverty guideline for a family of four, $32,150 (48 contiguous states). Money flowing through industries: BEA Gross Output by Industry via FRED (all-industry gross output ≈$53T, 2025) — M2 is a money stock; gross output and GDP are annual flows.
  • Pick your state (the state picker): GDP by industry by state from BEA SAGDP2 (nominal, 2025); jobs & pay by industry by state from BLS QCEW (2025). Private industries are private-sector; "Government" is federal + state + local combined. State figures may differ slightly from the national charts (the private/government split).
  • Question 3 (profits — cents kept): Aswath Damodaran (NYU Stern), Margins by Sector, Jan 2026 (US-listed firms).
  • Question 4 (what's “worth” — value & taxes): “Value added” = each industry's contribution to GDP (BEA, GDP by Industry). Family paycheck split from the BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey, 2023 — income before taxes $101,805, after taxes $87,869 (income taxes ≈$13,936), spending $77,280. Kept whole-vintage 2023 (not 2024) because BLS paused its tax estimate in the 2024 release — every piece here is a direct published figure, no estimates.
  • Question 5 (the family budget & the $1,000 game): BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey, 2024 ($78,535 average household spending). Consumer spending ≈68% of GDP: BEA NIPA table 1.1.10 (FRED DPCERE1Q156NBEA, 2025 quarterly average).
  • Question 6 (spending over 40 years): BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey historical tables, 1984–2024 — each category's share of spending, and the dollars, per year.
  • Question 7 (spending by age & Engel's law): BLS Consumer Expenditure Survey, 2024 — Table 1300 (by age of reference person) and Table 1101 (quintiles of income before taxes).
  • Question 8 (spending where you live): BEA Personal Consumption Expenditures by State, 2024 — per-person totals (SAPCE2) and category detail (SAPCE3).
  • Question 9 (supply vs. the crowd): population from FRED POPTHM; housing units from the Census Housing Vacancy Survey (FRED ETOTALUSQ176N); registered vehicles from FHWA Highway Statistics MV-1 (through 2023); hospital beds per 1,000 people (including the 1980 ≈6-per-1,000 figure) from the World Bank / OECD (through 2022).
  • Background cake artwork: the 🍰 slices are from Twemoji, © Twitter/X & contributors, licensed CC-BY 4.0.

Thanks & inspiration

This kid-friendly explainer was inspired by, and learned from, a mix of journalism, research and AI conversations. Order does not imply rank.

  • The Free Press
  • Bloomberg
  • Public Policy Institute of California (PPIC)
  • the Hapitalist channel on Substack
  • the a16z Podcast (Andreessen Horowitz)
  • the Federal Reserve and the U.S. Treasury websites
  • conversations with Copilot, Gemini, DeepSeek and ChatGPT

These are for inspiration and background reading only; every figure in this report comes from the sources listed above, and any errors are ours.

For grown-ups: This is a friendly explainer, not investment, financial, or legal advice. Figures are from third-party sources believed reliable but may be revised. The full source list, data vintages, and cross-checks live beside this page in data/ (its README.md and sources.md).
